paper_title: A Measure of Countries' Distance to Frontier Based on Comparative Advantage
research_answer: Yes, and the pattern is highly systematic. Countries closer to the global technological frontier are not just better at producing complex products — they are relatively more productive in complex products in a compounding, non-linear way. This property, known as log-supermodularity, means that moving from a simpler to a more complex product increases productivity proportionally more for frontier countries than for less advanced ones. The country-country similarity matrix shows this pattern holds 74.1% of the time at the individual country level across 130 countries, and 95.6% when countries are grouped into 13 bins by capability. Similarly, the product-product matrix shows 84.6% log-supermodularity across 243 product bins. This structure is the theoretical foundation for why export composition — and not just export volume — matters for economic development.
supporting_quotes: Figure 5 shows that productivities are log-supermodular, with countries closer to the frontier having a relatively—in a diff-in-diff sense—higher productivity in more complex products.
As may be seen from Table 3, these tests strongly indicate that the estimated country-country similarity matrix is indeed log-supermodular.
Table S6 summarizes a test of log-supermodularity following Davis and Dingel (2020) for the product-product similarity matrix
